0.summary: The initial position. White is about to begin the Jobava London.
0.why: Select a move in the tree to see its rationale.
0.logic: The plan: 1.d4, then Nc3 and Bf4 — active, attacking London chess.
plans: Play 1.d4 and aim for the Nc3 + Bf4 setup.
0.hook: d4, Nc3, Bf4 — the Jobava skeleton.

1.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on d4. Development: White has 0 minor piece(s) out, Black 0. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is Black's move.
1.why: 1.d4 advances a pawn to d4. It grabs the centre and opens lines for the queen and dark-squared bishop, the standard first step of a queen's-pawn opening.
1.logic: Every Jobava London starts here: claim the centre, then develop with Nc3 and Bf4 rather than the slow c3 London.
1.prevents: More a constructive move than a preventive one: it denies the opponent easy targets and keeps the position free of weaknesses.
1.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Develop quickly with Nc3 and Bf4 (the Jobava move order).
Keep the e4 break in mind once pieces are out.
Decide later between a kingside storm (h4-h5) and quiet short castling.
1.hook: 1.d4 then Nc3+Bf4 — that's the Jobava, not the slow c3 London.
1.mistake: Don't drift: keep developing and castle before starting an attack, and don't push pawns in front of your own king without a reason.

2.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on d4, Black pawn on d5. Development: White has 0 minor piece(s) out, Black 0. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is White's move.

2.logic: This move supports the core Jobava London plan: rapid development with Nc3 and Bf4, pressure on c7/d5, and the option to break with e4 or launch the h-pawn depending on where Black puts the king. Against d5, the repertoire continues 2.Nc3.
2.prevents: More a constructive move than a preventive one: it denies the opponent easy targets and keeps the position free of weaknesses.

3.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on d4, Black pawn on d5. Development: White has 1 minor piece(s) out, Black 0. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is Black's move.
3.why: 2.Nc3 develops/repositions the knight to c3. This is the move that defines the Jobava London. The knight eyes d5, e4 and b5, and (unlike the slow c3 London) keeps White ready for a quick e4 break.
3.logic: Nc3 + Bf4 is the signature. The knight on c3 (not pinned by ...Bb4 yet) keeps e4 in the air and enables the Nb5 jab at c7/d6 — the whole point of the system.
3.prevents: It keeps e4 and Nb5 available, denying Black an easy, comfortable equalising setup.
3.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Bring the bishop to f4 next, before playing e3.
Keep Nb5 in reserve to hit c7/d6 if Black is careless.
Prepare e4 to blow open the centre while ahead in development.
3.hook: Knight to c3 first: it's the move that makes the London bite.
3.mistake: Don't reflexively block the c-pawn worries — in the Jobava, Nc3 is correct precisely because you want e4 and Nb5, not c2-c4.

5.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on d4, Black pawn on d5. Development: White has 2 minor piece(s) out, Black 1. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is Black's move.
5.why: 3.Bf4 develops/repositions the bishop to f4. The bishop comes out before e3, so it is active outside the pawn chain. From f4 it pressures c7 and supports the whole queenside plan.
5.logic: Bf4 before e3 is the London bishop done right: active, outside the pawn chain, and aimed at c7. Combined with Nc3 it sets up Nb5 and e4 ideas.
5.prevents: Developing the bishop now stops it getting locked behind an e3-pawn (the classic 'bad London bishop' problem).
5.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Play e3 to free the f1-bishop, then develop the knight (f3 or e2) and castle.
Eye Nb5 — if Black allows it, the knight jumps in with tempo.
Against ...Bf5/...g6, switch to the f3 + g4 + h4 expansion.
5.hook: Bishop OUT to f4 before e3 — never bury the London bishop.
5.mistake: Don't play e3 before Bf4, or the bishop gets locked in. Bishop first!

7.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on d4, Black pawn on d5. Development: White has 2 minor piece(s) out, Black 2. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is Black's move.
7.why: 4.Nb5 develops/repositions the knight to b5. The thematic jump. The knight hits c7 (forking ideas with the f4-bishop) and d6, often forcing Black to make a concession or allow Nxc7/Nxd6.
7.logic: Nb5 is the tactical heartbeat of the Jobava London: it attacks c7 (with the f4-bishop) and d6, forcing concessions. Often it wins the bishop pair or a pawn.
7.prevents: It punishes Black for leaving c7 and d6 loose and prevents a comfortable ...Bd6 or ...e5 setup.
7.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: If Black defends c7, consider Nxd6 to grab the bishop pair, or retreat with a gain.
Combine the knight with Bf4 and the queen to keep hitting c7.
Open the position with dxe5 / e4 while your pieces are the more active.
7.hook: See c7 loose? Nb5! — the Jobava's signature jab.
7.mistake: Don't play Nb5 just for show — check that c7/d6 are genuinely loose; otherwise the knight gets chased by ...a6 with tempo.

8.logic: ...e5 is the critical central try; the repertoire answers concretely (dxe5 and tactics on c7/d5) because Black is behind in development. Against e5, the repertoire continues 5.dxe5.
8.prevents: By striking early Black tries to free the position before White's pieces dominate — the repertoire reacts before that happens.

57.why: 5.Ne2 develops/repositions the knight to e2. A flexible square: the knight avoids any ...Bb4 pin, keeps the f3-pawn or g4 thrust available, and can later reroute to g3 or f4.
57.logic: Choosing Ne2 over Nf3 keeps the f-pawn free for f3/g4 and sidesteps pins — a more aggressive, kingside-storming flavour of the system.
57.prevents: Developing to e2 sidesteps the ...Bb4 pin entirely and keeps f3/g4 available.
57.tactics: Checks for Black: Bxc3+. Pinned White pieces: knight on c3.
plans: Finish developing: e3, a knight to f3 or e2, the light-squared bishop, then castle.
Look for the e4 break to open the centre once your pieces are ready.
Watch for Nb5 ideas hitting c7 and d6 if Black neglects that square.
57.hook: Ne2 dodges ...Bb4 and keeps g4 in the holster.
57.mistake: Don't drift: keep developing and castle before starting an attack, and don't push pawns in front of your own king without a reason.

61.logic: g4 is part of White's kingside pawn storm. Against ...Bf5/...Bg6 it gains space and chases the bishop, frequently followed by h4-h5.
61.prevents: It denies the f5/g6 squares to Black's bishop and gains space before Black can complete a solid setup.
61.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.

Castle queenside (Qd2, O-O-O) so your king is safe during the storm.
Target the chased bishop and the weakened light squares.
61.hook: g4 kicks the bishop and screams 'pawn storm!'
61.mistake: Don't launch g4/h4 with your own king still in the centre — make sure you can castle queenside or your king is safe first.

63.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on d4, Black pawn on d5. Development: White has 2 minor piece(s) out, Black 1. 63.why: 4.Nb5 develops/repositions the knight to b5. The thematic jump. The knight hits c7 (forking ideas with the f4-bishop) and d6, often forcing Black to make a concession or allow Nxc7/Nxd6.
63.logic: Nb5 is the tactical heartbeat of the Jobava London: it attacks c7 (with the f4-bishop) and d6, forcing concessions. Often it wins the bishop pair or a pawn.
63.prevents: It punishes Black for leaving c7 and d6 loose and prevents a comfortable ...Bd6 or ...e5 setup.
plans: If Black defends c7, consider Nxd6 to grab the bishop pair, or retreat with a gain.
Combine the knight with Bf4 and the queen to keep hitting c7.
Open the position with dxe5 / e4 while your pieces are the more active.
63.hook: See c7 loose? Nb5! — the Jobava's signature jab.
63.mistake: Don't play Nb5 just for show — check that c7/d6 are genuinely loose; otherwise the knight gets chased by ...a6 with tempo.

125.summary: Material favours White by 1 point(s). Central pawns: White pawn on e5. Development: White has 2 minor piece(s) out, Black 1. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure: White has doubled pawns on the e-file. It is Black's move.
125.why: 4.Nc3 develops/repositions the knight to c3. This is the move that defines the Jobava London. The knight eyes d5, e4 and b5, and (unlike the slow c3 London) keeps White ready for a quick e4 break.
125.logic: Nc3 + Bf4 is the signature. The knight on c3 (not pinned by ...Bb4 yet) keeps e4 in the air and enables the Nb5 jab at c7/d6 — the whole point of the system.
125.prevents: It keeps e4 and Nb5 available, denying Black an easy, comfortable equalising setup.
125.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Bring the bishop to f4 next, before playing e3.
Keep Nb5 in reserve to hit c7/d6 if Black is careless.
Prepare e4 to blow open the centre while ahead in development.
125.hook: Knight to c3 first: it's the move that makes the London bite.
125.mistake: Don't reflexively block the c-pawn worries — in the Jobava, Nc3 is correct precisely because you want e4 and Nb5, not c2-c4.

135.mistake: Don't reflexively block the c-pawn worries — in the Jobava, Nc3 is correct precisely because you want e4 and Nb5, not c2-c4.

171.mistake: Don't launch g4/h4 with your own king still in the centre — make sure you can castle queenside or your king is safe first.

213.why: 6.h4 advances a pawn to h4. A direct pawn-storm move. With Black committed on the kingside, White rolls the h-pawn to pry open lines in front of the enemy king.
213.logic: h4-h5 is the attacking soul of the aggressive Jobava: roll the rook's pawn at Black's king, especially after ...g6 or opposite-side castling.
213.prevents: More a constructive move than a preventive one: it denies the opponent easy targets and keeps the position free of weaknesses.
213.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Finish developing: e3, a knight to f3 or e2, the light-squared bishop, then castle.
Look for the e4 break to open the centre once your pieces are ready.
Watch for Nb5 ideas hitting c7 and d6 if Black neglects that square.
213.hook: h4-h5: roll the rook's pawn at the enemy king.
213.mistake: A pawn storm needs your king tucked away on the other wing; storming with a king in the centre often backfires.

235.hook: Bishop OUT to f4 before e3 — never bury the London bishop.
235.mistake: Don't play e3 before Bf4, or the bishop gets locked in. Bishop first!

252.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Finish developing: e3, a knight to f3 or e2, the light-squared bishop, then castle.
Look for the e4 break to open the centre once your pieces are ready.
Watch for Nb5 ideas hitting c7 and d6 if Black neglects that square.
252.hook: Remember 1...b6 as a natural building move in the Jobava London setup.
252.mistake: Don't drift: keep developing and castle before starting an attack, and don't push pawns in front of your own king without a reason.

253.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on d4, White pawn on e4. Development: White has 0 minor piece(s) out, Black 0. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is Black's move.
253.why: 2.e4 advances a pawn to e4. The key Jobava break. Backed by Nc3 and Bf4, White strikes in the centre to open lines while ahead in development.
253.logic: The e4 break is what separates the Jobava from the dull London: with Nc3 and Bf4 already developed, White opens the centre to attack.
253.prevents: More a constructive move than a preventive one: it denies the opponent easy targets and keeps the position free of weaknesses.
253.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Open the centre and use your lead in development.
Bring rooks to the central files (d- and e-files).
Look for tactics against Black's slightly exposed pieces.
253.hook: e4 is the Jobava punch — the London that actually attacks.
253.mistake: Don't rush e4 before you're developed; premature in the centre, it just opens lines for the opponent.

254.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on d4. Development: White has 0 minor piece(s) out, Black 0. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is White's move.
254.why: 1...f5 advances a pawn to f5. It is a natural, healthy move that improves a piece or the pawn structure and keeps the position sound and flexible.
254.logic: This move supports the core Jobava London plan: rapid development with Nc3 and Bf4, pressure on c7/d5, and the option to break with e4 or launch the h-pawn depending on where Black puts the king. Against f5, the repertoire continues 2.Nc3.
254.prevents: More a constructive move than a preventive one: it denies the opponent easy targets and keeps the position free of weaknesses.

255.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on d4. Development: White has 1 minor piece(s) out, Black 0. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is Black's move.
255.why: 2.Nc3 develops/repositions the knight to c3. This is the move that defines the Jobava London. The knight eyes d5, e4 and b5, and (unlike the slow c3 London) keeps White ready for a quick e4 break.
255.logic: Nc3 + Bf4 is the signature. The knight on c3 (not pinned by ...Bb4 yet) keeps e4 in the air and enables the Nb5 jab at c7/d6 — the whole point of the system.
255.prevents: It keeps e4 and Nb5 available, denying Black an easy, comfortable equalising setup.
255.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Bring the bishop to f4 next, before playing e3.
Keep Nb5 in reserve to hit c7/d6 if Black is careless.
Prepare e4 to blow open the centre while ahead in development.
255.hook: Knight to c3 first: it's the move that makes the London bite.
255.mistake: Don't reflexively block the c-pawn worries — in the Jobava, Nc3 is correct precisely because you want e4 and Nb5, not c2-c4.

260.summary: Material is level. Central pawns: White pawn on d4. Development: White has 2 minor piece(s) out, Black 1. Kings — White is still in the centre (can still castle); Black is still in the centre (can still castle). Pawn structure is still healthy and symmetrical-ish. It is Black's move.
260.why: 3.Bf4 develops/repositions the bishop to f4. The bishop comes out before e3, so it is active outside the pawn chain. From f4 it pressures c7 and supports the whole queenside plan.
260.logic: Bf4 before e3 is the London bishop done right: active, outside the pawn chain, and aimed at c7. Combined with Nc3 it sets up Nb5 and e4 ideas.
260.prevents: Developing the bishop now stops it getting locked behind an e3-pawn (the classic 'bad London bishop' problem).

alts.idea: The key Jobava break.
alts.why_not_preferred: Another branch the repertoire prepares for.

260.tactics: No immediate forcing tactics: no checks, no winning captures and no loose pieces. The position is calm and about plans, not tricks.
plans: Play e3 to free the f1-bishop, then develop the knight (f3 or e2) and castle.
Eye Nb5 — if Black allows it, the knight jumps in with tempo.
Against ...Bf5/...g6, switch to the f3 + g4 + h4 expansion.
260.hook: Bishop OUT to f4 before e3 — never bury the London bishop.
260.mistake: Don't play e3 before Bf4, or the bishop gets locked in. Bishop first!
